opp.ais_why:
• Champion Camilo acknowledged that a system like this is the present and future, but his stated needs are generic — process automation, supplier management, compliance — with no Merlin, ANA, or agentic language.
• No Avoma meetings are tied to this opp, so there is no transcript evidence to test the AI thesis.
• Next step is sending a brochure and SAP integration approach with a steel-industry case study — exploratory stage with no AI excitement signal.

sources: Avoma discovery call 2026-04-17, Camilo Sierra Ortega
Avoma notes: 'RFQs and quotations are managed via emails, chats, and phone calls, causing version control issues' (2026-04-17)
Avoma notes: 'Supplier onboarding is manual via email and PDF; a self-service portal for onboarding is required' (2026-04-17)
metrics.narrative: Camilo Sierra Ortega (Procurement Leader) articulated two operational pain points on the 2026-04-17 discovery call: (1) RFQs and quotations managed via emails, chats, and phone calls, causing version control issues (quote: 'RFQs and quotations are managed via emails, chats, and phone calls, causing version control issues', source: Avoma notes 2026-04-17), and (2) manual supplier onboarding via email and PDF with no self-service portal (quote: 'Supplier onboarding is manual via email and PDF; a self-service portal for onboarding is required', source: Avoma notes 2026-04-17). However, no quantified business impact was stated: no cost of manual process, no cycle-time delay, no error rate, no headcount burden, and no savings/ROI target. The buyer described the what (manual, version control problems) but not the quantified why (financial or operational magnitude). The Salesforce value-case fields (Customer_Business_Problem__c, Business_Objectives__c, Value_to_Customer__c) were not read in the available queries and may not exist in this org. Next move: quantify the pain by asking Camilo how many RFQs per month, average cycle time, and the cost of manual supplier onboarding (time, errors, delays).

sources: Avoma discovery call 2026-04-17: Camilo Sierra Ortega led the call, articulated pain, asked about SAP integration
Salesforce Next_Step: deliverables owed to Camilo (brochure, integration approach, case study)
No follow-on engagement logged since 2026-04-17 (70 days ago)
champion.narrative: Camilo Sierra Ortega (Procurement Leader) is the single point of contact and the most likely champion candidate. He led the discovery call on 2026-04-17, articulated the operational pain (RFQ chaos, manual supplier onboarding), and asked about SAP integration, demonstrating ownership of the problem and interest in Zycus as a solution. However, there is no evidence of championship behavior: no introduction to the Economic Buyer or Decision Maker, no internal advocacy (sharing our materials internally, pushing back on competitors, driving the agenda), and no follow-on engagement in the 70 days since the call. Camilo is engaged but passive: he requested deliverables (brochure, SAP integration approach, steel case study) and is waiting for us to follow through, but he has not proactively advanced the deal. The strength is 'developing' at best: he is the operational pain owner and the primary contact, but he has not demonstrated access to budget authority (CFO Adalberto Contreras has not appeared), has not multi-threaded us to other stakeholders (Nicholas Harrison and Isabel Pulgarin attended the discovery call but have not re-engaged), and has not taken any action to move the deal forward on his side. The champion is at risk of cooling: 70 days of silence after a single discovery call, with no follow-on meeting scheduled, suggests the relationship is thinning. Next move: deliver the promised materials immediately, then schedule a follow-up call with Camilo to (1) validate fit against the pain points, (2) ask him to introduce us to the CFO or budget approver, and (3) co-author a lightweight evaluation plan with named next steps and dates.

sources: SF Competitors__c: SAP Ariba
Avoma discovery call 2026-04-17: no competitor mentioned by buyer
Buyer asked about SAP integration (wedge: integration depth)
Products in scope include Merlin and Merlin Intake (wedge: AI differentiation)
Buyer requested steel industry case study (wedge: industry fit)
competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the named competitor (source: SF Competitors__c). No other competitor was mentioned on the discovery call or in any Salesforce field, Next_Step, or task. The buyer did not state they are evaluating Ariba or any other vendor during the 2026-04-17 discovery call; the Ariba entry in Salesforce may be a standing assumption (Ariba is the incumbent or the known peer in this space) rather than active competitive intelligence. The discovery call focused on Optimus Steel's current state (SAP ERP, manual RFQ/supplier processes) and how Zycus integrates, with no discussion of other vendors, shortlists, or RFP rounds. The competitive read is thin: we know Ariba is the named rival, but we do not know threat level (are they actively evaluating Ariba? is Ariba the incumbent? has Ariba priced the deal?), status (active vs dormant), or the buyer's sentiment toward Ariba. The wedge: Zycus wins on SAP integration depth (the buyer explicitly asked about it, signaling it is a decision criterion), AI/Merlin positioning (Merlin Intake and negotiation agent are in scope and differentiate vs Ariba), and steel-industry fit (the buyer requested a steel case study, signaling industry reference matters). However, none of these wedges have been activated yet because we have not delivered the promised materials or held a follow-on demo. Next move: in the follow-up call after delivering the brochure and case study, ask Camilo directly: 'Are you evaluating other vendors alongside Zycus, and if so, who?' and 'What is your experience with Ariba or other procurement platforms?' to confirm competitive reality.

sources: Avoma notes 2026-04-17, Camilo Sierra Ortega: 'RFQs and quotations are managed via emails, chats, and phone calls, causing version control issues'
Avoma notes 2026-04-17, Camilo Sierra Ortega: 'Supplier onboarding is manual via email and PDF; a self-service portal for onboarding is required'
Avoma transcript 2026-04-17: buyer asked 'So How how Zycus connect to, SAP?' (integration concern)
identify_pain.narrative: Camilo Sierra Ortega (Procurement Leader) owns and articulated two specific operational pains on the 2026-04-17 discovery call. Pain 1: RFQs and quotations are managed via emails, chats, and phone calls, causing version control issues (quote: 'RFQs and quotations are managed via emails, chats, and phone calls, causing version control issues', source: Avoma notes 2026-04-17). This is confirmed pain, stated directly by the buyer. Pain 2: Supplier onboarding is manual via email and PDF with no self-service portal (quote: 'Supplier onboarding is manual via email and PDF; a self-service portal for onboarding is required', source: Avoma notes 2026-04-17). This is also confirmed pain, buyer-initiated. Both pains are operational (process inefficiency, manual work, version control chaos) and tie directly to the modules in scope (iSource for RFQ management, iSupplier for supplier onboarding). The pain is clear and owned by the procurement function, but not yet quantified (no cost, no cycle time, no error rate stated). The buyer also described their current ERP (SAP) and asked how Zycus integrates, signaling that ERP integration complexity is an implied pain point (replacing a working system must be seamless). No financial or executive-level pain (e.g., cost overruns, compliance risk, strategic sourcing goals) has been articulated, which is a gap given the $150k investment and the need for CFO/executive sponsorship.

sources: Avoma discovery call 2026-04-17: no contracting, legal, InfoSec, or SI discussion
No subsequent calls or tasks addressing paper process
paper_process.narrative: No contracting, legal, InfoSec, or procurement-channel discussion has occurred. The discovery call on 2026-04-17 focused on operational pain (RFQ management, supplier onboarding) and system integration (SAP), but did not touch MSA, SOW, legal review, InfoSec requirements, or implementation scoping. No SI or partner involvement has been mentioned. The Salesforce contracting fields (Current_Contract_Expiration__c as a forcing function) were not retrieved in the available SOQL queries and may not exist in this org. No paper-process signal exists in any call, Next_Step, or task. The deal is in Qualified stage, 279 days from close, so paper-process mapping is not yet urgent, but understanding whether Optimus Steel has a standard procurement channel (e.g., centralized procurement in the US parent company Turia Group vs local Colombia procurement), whether InfoSec will gate the deal, and whether an SI will be involved in implementation is critical before advancing to the relevant stakeholder or Negotiation. Next move: once the discovery deliverables are sent and the buyer re-engages, ask Camilo who handles vendor contracting (legal, procurement, InfoSec) and whether any third party (SI, Turia Group corporate) will be involved in the evaluation or contracting process.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified. The discovery call on 2026-04-17 included three procurement-function contacts (Camilo Sierra Ortega, Procurement Leader; Nicholas Harrison, Manager, Procurement; Isabel Pulgarin, Procurement), but no CFO, VP Finance, or C-level executive participated. The account-level contact list shows Adalberto Contreras as Chief Financial Officer, but he has not appeared on any call, task, or event, so his involvement in THIS opportunity is unconfirmed. No Salesforce Contact Role of Economic Buyer or Executive Sponsor exists on this opportunity. The Salesforce fields that typically encode EB data (X10a_Sponsor__c, Who_will_approve_budget__c, Purchase_approvals_Required_from__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c, Executive_Sponsor_Identified__c) were not retrieved in the available SOQL queries and may not exist in this org. The discovery call focused on operational pain and system integration (SAP), but no commercial or pricing conversation occurred, and no budget authority was discussed. Budget approval and CFO sponsorship remain unmapped. Next move: ask Camilo Sierra Ortega who will approve the $150k investment and request an introduction to that person, likely the CFO or a VP-level sponsor.

sources: Avoma discovery call 2026-04-17 attendee list: procurement roles only, no executive or finance participation
No subsequent calls, no escalation events logged since 2026-04-17
decision_process.narrative: No decision process has been mapped. The single discovery call on 2026-04-17 involved three procurement-function contacts (Camilo Sierra Ortega, Nicholas Harrison, Isabel Pulgarin), all from the operational layer, with no executive or finance participation. No subsequent escalation, executive briefing, or committee formation has occurred. The call sequence itself shows no progression: one discovery call, then 70 days of silence. The Salesforce process fields (What_is_the_decision_process__c, Mandate__c, Does_the_Buyer_need_approval__c, Purchase_approvals_Required_from__c, Multiple_approvals__c) were not retrieved in the available SOQL queries and may not exist in this org. No approval chain, vendor selection committee, or timeline for shortlisting/RFP has been discussed. The buyer has not articulated next steps beyond receiving our deliverables (brochure, SAP integration approach, case study). It is unclear whether Optimus Steel will run a formal RFI/RFP, how many vendors they are evaluating, or who will make the final decision. Next move: ask Camilo Sierra Ortega to map out the decision process (who needs to approve, how many vendors are being considered, what the evaluation timeline looks like, and when they expect to make a decision) during the follow-up call after we deliver the promised materials.

sources: Avoma notes 2026-04-17: 'RFQs and quotations are managed via emails, chats, and phone calls, causing version control issues'

decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer articulated two primary evaluation criteria on the 2026-04-17 discovery call, both inferred from the pain points rather than stated as formal criteria: (1) ability to replace email/chat/phone RFQ management with a structured system that solves version control issues, and (2) a self-service supplier onboarding portal to eliminate manual email/PDF onboarding (source: Avoma notes 2026-04-17). The buyer also explicitly asked how Zycus integrates with SAP (quote: 'So How how Zycus connect to, SAP?', speaker: Camilo Sierra Ortega or Nicholas Harrison, 2026-04-17), signaling that SAP integration depth is a decision criterion. However, no formal RFI, RFP, or BRD has been issued, and no scoring rubric, use-case validation, or structured evaluation process has been discussed. The Salesforce decision-criteria fields (Business_Requirements__c, Top_Challenges_Priorities__c, AI_Needs_in_RFP_Rating__c, What_is_the_decision_process__c) were not retrieved in the available SOQL queries and may not exist in this org. The criteria are inferred from pain, not confirmed through a formal evaluation framework. Next move: co-author a lightweight evaluation checklist with Camilo that names the must-haves (SAP integration, RFQ workflow, supplier portal, module fit) and use it to anchor the next demo.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Merlin and Merlin Intake are in scope (source: SF Products__c), and the buyer's pain (manual RFQ/supplier processes, version control chaos) is a strong fit for AI-driven automation, signaling 'AI Curious' tier. However, no AIS score exists, and no AI-specific conversation occurred on the discovery call. The buyer has not explicitly stated AI appetite or AI-driven use cases, so the tier is inferred from the pain (manual processes that AI solves) and the product scope (Merlin in scope). The buyer requested a steel industry case study, which is an opportunity to showcase AI-driven procurement transformation in a peer steel manufacturer. AI fit is latent but not yet activated.

confidence_signals.summary: Mixed signals: strong pain articulation (RFQ chaos, manual supplier onboarding) and product-scope fit, but momentum has stalled due to our inaction on committed deliverables. The buyer is engaged but passive, waiting for us to follow through. The relationship is cooling: 70 days since the discovery call with no follow-on meeting, no multi-threading, and no executive engagement. Confidence is 'developing' but at risk of fading if we do not re-engage immediately.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 2026-04-17, qualified same day (70 days ago). Close date 2027-04-01 (279 days out). Single discovery call 2026-04-17 (70 days ago). LastActivityDate 2026-05-21 (36 days ago, rep-side activity). Time-in-Qualified: 70 days. Normal early-stage pace allows multi-month gaps between formal events, but the buyer explicitly requested deliverables on 2026-04-17 (brochure, integration approach, steel case study) and we have not yet delivered them 70 days later. Buyer last engaged 70 days ago; rep last touched the deal 36 days ago. No multi-threading beyond the initial three contacts (Camilo Sierra Ortega, Nicholas Harrison, Isabel Pulgarin). No follow-on demo, RFI, or deeper engagement scheduled. The deal is not cold (less than 60 days since rep activity), but momentum has clearly thinned: we owe three concrete deliverables from the discovery call and have not executed them, and no buyer-facing event has occurred since the kickoff.
evidence: Single discovery call 2026-04-17 (70 days ago) with Camilo Sierra Ortega (Procurement Leader), Nicholas Harrison (Manager, Procurement), Isabel Pulgarin (Procurement)

north_star_verdict.headline: Qualified-stage deal stalled 70 days post-discovery with zero buyer engagement; seller has failed to deliver three explicit commitments (brochure, SAP integration approach, steel case study) blocking any progression to next evaluation milestone.

best_practice_check.summary: Early-stage deal (Qualified, 70 days old) with healthy pain articulation but three critical execution gaps: (1) committed deliverables from discovery call remain undelivered 70 days later, stalling buyer engagement; (2) no multi-threading beyond procurement function (no CFO/EB, no IT, no executive sponsor) on a $150k investment; (3) no champion development or access to budget authority. The buyer is waiting for us to follow through; momentum hinges on executing the promised next steps immediately.

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is the named competitor (source: SF Competitors__c), but competitive reality is thin: no active evaluation, shortlist, or pricing discussion has occurred, and Ariba was not mentioned by the buyer on the discovery call. Threat level is currently LOW due to lack of active competitive signal, but this is a knowledge gap, not a confirmed advantage. If Optimus Steel runs a formal RFP or shortlist, Ariba will likely be invited (it is the known peer in procurement software). Zycus wedge: SAP integration depth (buyer explicitly asked about it), AI/Merlin differentiation (Merlin Intake and negotiation agent in scope), and steel-industry fit (buyer requested a steel case study). However, none of these wedges have been activated yet because we have not delivered the promised materials or held a follow-on demo. The single strongest current threat is DO-NOTHING: the deal has been silent for 70 days after the discovery call, and the buyer may deprioritize the procurement transformation if we do not re-engage and demonstrate value quickly.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Merlin and Merlin Intake are in scope (source: SF Products__c), signaling AI appetite, but AI positioning has not been activated. No AI-specific discussion occurred on the discovery call, no AIS score exists, and the buyer has not articulated AI-driven use cases (e.g., intake automation, negotiation agent, spend analytics). The steel industry context (manual processes, version control issues, supplier onboarding chaos) is a strong fit for AI-driven automation, but we have not yet made the case. AI positioning is under-positioned: the modules are in scope, but the value story has not been told. Next move: in the follow-up demo after delivering the discovery materials, lead with Merlin Intake (automate RFQ intake and supplier onboarding, the exact pain points the buyer articulated) and the negotiation agent (demonstrate AI-driven supplier price optimization on steel procurement use cases).

items.criterion: Self-service supplier onboarding portal to eliminate manual email/PDF onboarding

items.quote: So How how Zycus connect to, SAP?
items.criterion: SAP integration depth (buyer concern: how does Zycus connect to SAP)

items.quote: Verify whether a Zycus case study exists for a steel mill customer; if available, send it to Optimus Steel, or else arrange an introduction with a steel mill reference
items.criterion: Steel industry reference and fit (buyer requested steel mill case study)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Optimus Steel's stated pain (RFQ email chaos, manual supplier onboarding, SAP integration concern) aligns well with the modules in scope (iSource for RFQ management, iSupplier for supplier portal, SAP integration is standard Zycus strength), but fit has not been validated through a demo or use-case review. The buyer requested a steel industry case study, signaling that industry-specific reference is a decision criterion. Products in scope (iContract, iSource, iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin, Merlin Intake) cover the full S2P cycle and include AI differentiation (Merlin Intake for intake automation, Merlin negotiation agent), which is a strong fit for a steel manufacturer looking to modernize procurement. However, we have not yet demonstrated how these modules solve the buyer's specific pain points or validated that the SAP integration approach meets their requirements. Fit is 'partially aligned' pending a deeper demo and use-case validation.

hard.ais_why:
• Champion Camilo acknowledged that a system like this is the present and future, but his stated needs are generic — process automation, supplier management, compliance — with no Merlin, ANA, or agentic language.
• No Avoma meetings are tied to this opp, so there is no transcript evidence to test the AI thesis.
• Next step is sending a brochure and SAP integration approach with a steel-industry case study — exploratory stage with no AI excitement signal.
hard.next_step: Bailey : Send brochure/deck with module breakdown, integration approach with SAP, and relevant steel industry case study if available.

pulse.summary: Live: buyer call(s) read this sweep.
